Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.title: Explain Social Identity Theory with reference to one study.
Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.content:
Introduction
- Social Identity Theory (SIT), proposed by Tajfel and Turner (1979), explains how individuals drive part of their self-concept from the social groups to which they belong.
- According to the theory, people first engage in social categorization, classifying themselves and others into groups, distinguishing between “in-groups” (us) and “out-groups” (them).
- This is followed by social identification, where individuals adopt the norms, values, and behaviour of their in-group. 
Once group membership is established, individuals engage in social comparison, evaluating their in-group relative to out-groups in order to justify their group affiliation. 
- Through this process, people seek positive distinctiveness, enhancing their self-esteem by favouring their in-group over out-groups, often leading to in-group favouritism and out-group discrimination.
